question:
A $2 \times 3$ rectangle has vertices as $(0, 0), (2,0), (0,3),$ and $(2,
3)$. It rotates $90^\circ$ clockwise about the point $(2, 0)$. It then
rotates $90^\circ$ clockwise about the point $(5, 0)$, then $90^\circ$
clockwise about the point $(7, 0)$, and finally, $90^\circ$ clockwise
about the point $(10, 0)$. (The side originally on the $x$-axis is now
back on the $x$-axis.) Find the area of the region above the $x$-axis and
below the curve traced out by the point whose initial position is (1,1).
solution:
Solution.
figure 21.

The point \( (1,1) \) rotates around \( (2,0) \) to \( (3,1) \), then around \( (5,0) \) to \( (6,2) \), then around \( (7,0) \) to \( (9,1) \), then around \( (10,0) \) to \( (11,1) \). (See Figure 21.) The area of concern consists of four \( 1 \times 1 \) right triangles of area \( 1 / 2 \), four \( 1 \times 2 \) triangles of area 1 , two quarter circles of area \( (\pi / 4)(\sqrt{2})^{2}=\pi / 2 \), and two quarter circles of area \( (\pi / 4)(\sqrt{5})^{2}=5 \pi / 4 \), for a total area of \( 7 \pi / 2+6 \).

figure 21.

garbled_string.question:
A $2 \times 3$ rectangle has vertices as $(0, 0), (2,0), (0,3),$ and $(2,
3)$. It rotates $90^\circ$ clockwise about the point $(2, 0)$. It then
rotates $90^\circ$ clockwise about the point $(5, 0)$, then $90^\circ$
clockwise about the point $(7, 0)$, and finally, $90^\circ$ clockwise
about the point $(10, 0)$. (The side originally on the $qzxwvtnp$-axis is now
back on the $qzxwvtnp$-axis.) Find the area of the region above the $qzxwvtnp$-axis and
below the curve traced out by the point whose initial position is (1,1).
garbled_string.solution:
Solution.
figure 21.

The point \( (1,1) \) rotates around \( (2,0) \) to \( (3,1) \), then around \( (5,0) \) to \( (6,2) \), then around \( (7,0) \) to \( (9,1) \), then around \( (10,0) \) to \( (11,1) \). (See Figure 21.) The area of concern consists of four \( 1 \times 1 \) right triangles of area \( 1 / 2 \), four \( 1 \times 2 \) triangles of area 1 , two quarter circles of area \( (\pi / 4)(\sqrt{2})^{2}=\pi / 2 \), and two quarter circles of area \( (\pi / 4)(\sqrt{5})^{2}=5 \pi / 4 \), for a total area of \( 7 \pi / 2+6 \).

kernel_variant.question:
A $3\times4$ rectangle has vertices $(0,0),(3,0),(0,4)$ and $(3,4)$.  Beginning from this position it is subjected to six successive $90^{\circ}$ clockwise rotations about the points
$$\bigl(3,0\bigr),\;\bigl(7,0\bigr),\;\bigl(10,0\bigr),\;\bigl(14,0\bigr),\;\bigl(17,0\bigr),\;\text{and}\;\bigl(21,0\bigr)$$
on the $x$-axis.  Find the area of the region that lies above the $x$-axis and below the curve traced out by the point whose initial position is $(2,2)$.
kernel_variant.solution:
Let P_0=(2,2), and successive pivots O_1,\ldots ,O_6=(3,0),(7,0),(10,0),(14,0),(17,0),(21,0).  A 90^\circ clockwise rotation sends (x,y)\to (y,-x).  Tracking gives
 P_1=(5,1), P_2=(8,2), P_3=(12,2), P_4=(16,2), P_5=(19,1), P_6=(22,2).
The six arcs are quarter-circles of radii
 r_1,r_2,r_5,r_6=\sqrt{5},
 r_3,r_4=\sqrt{8}=2\sqrt{2},
 so \sum r_i^2=4\cdot 5+2\cdot 8=36.

Break the area under each arc into (a) area under its chord (a trapezoid) plus (b) the circular segment between chord and arc.  For arc i:
 * Trapezoid from x_{i-1} to x_i has heights y_{i-1},y_i and width \Delta x_i:  T_i=(y_{i-1}+y_i)/2\cdot \Delta x_i.
 * Segment area = sector minus central triangle = (\pi /4)r_i^2 - \frac{1}{2}r_i^2.

Compute trapezoids:
 \Delta x's =3,3,4,4,3,3;  (y_{i-1},y_i)=(2,1),(1,2),(2,2),(2,2),(2,1),(1,2)
 \Rightarrow   T's =4.5,4.5,8,8,4.5,4.5 \Rightarrow  \sum T=34.

Sum of segments = \sum [(\pi /4)r_i^2 - \frac{1}{2}r_i^2] = (\pi /4-\frac{1}{2})\cdot 36 = 9\pi  -18.

Total area = 34 + (9\pi  -18) = 16 + 9\pi .

core_steps: Track the point’s coordinates after each 90° clockwise rotation about the successive centers.
Realize each rotation moves the point along a quarter-circle whose center is that pivot and whose radius is the point-to-pivot distance at the start of that turn.
Note that the x-axis together with the endpoints of consecutive arcs encloses simple right triangles under every arc.
Compute the radii of the arcs and the legs of the triangles from the coordinate differences obtained in step 1.
Add the areas of all right triangles and quarter-circle sectors to get the required total.

slot1.description: Horizontal side-length of the starting rectangle

slot2.description: Vertical side-length of the starting rectangle

slot3.description: Initial point inside the rectangle (given relative to the lower-left corner)

slot4.description: Sequence of rotation centers on the x-axis

slot6.description: Radii of the quarter-circle arcs traced out (in the given order)

slot7.description: Leg lengths of the right triangles formed with the x-axis under successive arcs
